questions.question: A developer says Claude Code always enforces their team's commit-message format on their machine, but a new hire who cloned the same repo yesterday says Claude Code never mentions it. Both are using the same project. What is the most likely cause?
options: The convention lives in the first developer's `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, a user-level file that was never committed to the repo
The new hire needs to run `claude --sync-memory` after cloning
CLAUDE.md files require a manual `git submodule update` to take effect for new clones
The convention is defined in a `.claude/skills/` file that only the first developer has invoked
questions.explanation: User-level CLAUDE.md (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`) applies only to that user's machine and is never shared via version control. If a convention should apply to everyone who clones the repo, it belongs in the project-level `CLAUDE.md` (or `.claude/rules/`), not a personal file. There is no `--sync-memory` command.

questions.question: A monorepo has a root `CLAUDE.md` plus separate `packages/api/CONVENTIONS.md` and `packages/web/CONVENTIONS.md` files with package-specific standards. The team wants every session working in `packages/api` to automatically load only the API conventions, not the web conventions, while keeping the root file short. What's the appropriate mechanism?
options: Use `@import` in the root CLAUDE.md, or place the import inside a directory-level CLAUDE.md, so each package pulls in only its own conventions file
Paste the full contents of both CONVENTIONS.md files into the root CLAUDE.md under separate headers
Rename both CONVENTIONS.md files to CLAUDE.md and rely on Claude to infer which one is relevant
Move both files into `.claude/skills/` so they load only when explicitly invoked by name
questions.explanation: `@import` is the designed mechanism for pulling external files into CLAUDE.md selectively, keeping it modular. Pasting everything in defeats the purpose (option B) and bloats context for every session regardless of package. Renaming to CLAUDE.md doesn't scope anything by itself, and skills are for on-demand task procedures, not always-relevant package conventions.

questions.question: A teammate reports that Claude Code isn't following a documented convention while editing `src/routes/checkout.tsx`. You suspect a path-scoped rule exists but didn't activate. What is the fastest way to confirm which memory files actually loaded for that session?
options: Run `/memory` to list the loaded files and check whether the relevant rule's glob pattern actually matches the file being edited
Delete `.claude/rules/` and recreate it, since corrupted rule caches are the most common cause
Ask the teammate to restate the convention in the chat so Claude re-learns it for that session
Check the repository's GitHub Actions logs, since CLAUDE.md loading is logged there
questions.explanation: `/memory` is the built-in diagnostic for exactly this situation — it shows which memory files loaded, in hierarchy order, letting you check whether a glob simply didn't match. There's no such thing as a corrupted rule cache to reset, restating the convention in chat doesn't fix the underlying scoping problem, and CLAUDE.md loading isn't a CI-logged event.

questions.question: You're setting up a personal shortcut command for your own workflow — it summarizes your open PRs in a format only you find useful. It has no relevance to teammates and shouldn't show up when they run Claude Code. Where should it live?
options: `~/.claude/commands/` — a user-scoped command visible only in your own sessions
`.claude/commands/` — project-scoped so it's version-controlled
The project root `CLAUDE.md`, as a documented convention
A `.claude/skills/personal/` directory committed to the repo
questions.explanation: `~/.claude/commands/` is exactly for personal, unshared commands. Putting it in `.claude/commands/` (project-scoped) or committing anything to the repo (options C, D) would push a personal, irrelevant shortcut onto every teammate who clones the project.

questions.question: You're building a skill that runs a large-scale dependency audit — it reads dozens of files and produces pages of intermediate grep output before reaching a conclusion. Running it in the main conversation would consume most of the remaining context. What frontmatter field addresses this, and how?
options: `context: fork` — runs the skill in an isolated sub-agent context so only the final summary returns to the main conversation
`allowed-tools: [Grep]` — restricting tools reduces the amount of output produced
`argument-hint` — prompts the user to scope the audit to fewer files upfront
There is no frontmatter for this; the developer must manually summarize the skill's output afterward
questions.explanation: `context: fork` is specifically designed to isolate verbose output-producing skills into a sub-agent, returning only a distilled result. Restricting `allowed-tools` controls *which* tools can run, not how much output they produce, and `argument-hint` just documents expected arguments — it doesn't change context isolation.

questions.question: A team wants a skill that only ever proposes a migration plan as a written report — it must never be able to modify, delete, or execute anything in the repository, even if a bug in its own logic tried to. Which frontmatter field enforces this structurally, rather than just by instruction?
options: `allowed-tools`, restricted to read/write-report tools only (e.g. `[Read, Grep, Glob, Write]` scoped to a report file), excluding `Bash` and destructive edit tools
A prose instruction in the SKILL.md body telling the skill not to modify files
`context: fork`, since isolating the skill's context also isolates its tool access
`argument-hint`, since it constrains what arguments can be passed to the skill
questions.explanation: `allowed-tools` is the structural enforcement mechanism — a skill without `Bash` or destructive edit tools in its allow-list cannot execute or delete regardless of what its own reasoning concludes. A prose instruction is a request, not an enforcement mechanism (the exam consistently favors deterministic controls over prompt-only ones). `context: fork` isolates conversation context, not tool permissions, and `argument-hint` is unrelated to tool access.

questions.question: A `.claude/rules/formatting.md` file with no `paths` frontmatter sits alongside a `.claude/rules/terraform.md` file with `paths: ["infra/**/*"]`. What is the practical difference in when each loads?
options: `formatting.md` loads for every session regardless of what's being edited; `terraform.md` loads only when a file under `infra/` is being touched
Both load identically on every session, since `.claude/rules/` files are always loaded once present
Neither loads automatically — both require an explicit `/rules formatting` or `/rules terraform` invocation
`terraform.md` loads for every session, and `formatting.md` never loads because it's missing required frontmatter
questions.explanation: A rule file with no `paths` frontmatter is unscoped and always loads, like an additional CLAUDE.md section. A rule file with `paths` frontmatter loads conditionally, only when the current task touches a matching file — this is exactly what keeps irrelevant context (Terraform conventions during a frontend task) out of unrelated sessions.

questions.question: A codebase has three distinct areas — Python data pipelines, a Go backend service, and a set of Bash deployment scripts — each with different linting and error-handling conventions. Files of each type are not confined to one directory; Bash scripts in particular are scattered across `scripts/`, `deploy/`, and several per-service `bin/` directories. What is the most maintainable way to apply each area's conventions automatically?
options: Three `.claude/rules/` files, each scoped with a `paths` glob matching that language's file extension (e.g. `**/*.sh`) rather than a specific directory
One directory-level `CLAUDE.md` per top-level folder (`scripts/CLAUDE.md`, `deploy/CLAUDE.md`, etc.), duplicated wherever Bash scripts appear
A single root CLAUDE.md with three headers, relying on Claude to infer which section applies from file extension
Three separate skills, one per language, manually invoked whenever the relevant file type is being edited
questions.explanation: Glob-scoped rules key off file pattern, not directory, so `**/*.sh` applies consistently across `scripts/`, `deploy/`, and every service's `bin/` directory without duplication. Directory-level CLAUDE.md (B) can't cover files scattered across many directories without copy-pasting the same file repeatedly. A single file with headers (C) relies on inference rather than deterministic matching, and skills (D) require manual invocation rather than automatically applying as conventions are read.

questions.question: You need a rule that applies to any file matching `**/*.test.tsx` anywhere in the repository. Where does this rule's scoping information belong?
options: In the YAML frontmatter of a `.claude/rules/` file, as `paths: ["**/*.test.tsx"]`
In the file name itself — naming the rule file `test.tsx.md` is sufficient for Claude Code to scope it automatically
In a `scope:` key inside CLAUDE.md pointing at the rule file
Rules cannot be scoped by glob pattern; only by exact directory path
questions.explanation: Path scoping is expressed via a `paths` array in the rule file's YAML frontmatter, supporting glob patterns like `**/*.test.tsx`. File naming has no special scoping meaning, CLAUDE.md doesn't declare rule scope on a rule file's behalf, and glob patterns (not just exact directories) are explicitly supported.

questions.question: A request comes in: "Add a null check before this function dereferences `order.customer.id`, since the stack trace shows a crash there." What's the appropriate approach?
options: Direct execution — this is a well-scoped, single-function change with a clear, unambiguous fix
Plan mode — any bug fix should be planned before implementation to avoid rework
Delegate to the Explore subagent to survey the whole codebase for similar null-dereference risks before touching this function
Ask the user to choose between three possible null-handling strategies before proceeding
questions.explanation: This is exactly the well-understood, narrowly-scoped case direct execution is for — one function, one clear fix, no architectural ambiguity. Plan mode is reserved for complex, multi-file, or architecturally ambiguous work; invoking it here is unnecessary overhead. Surveying the whole codebase or presenting design options is disproportionate to a single guarded null check.

questions.question: A team wants to replace a deprecated logging library used inconsistently across ~60 files, with three call-site patterns that each need different replacement logic, and an open question of whether to do it in one pass or behind a compatibility shim. What is the most appropriate way to start?
options: Enter plan mode to survey the call-site patterns and decide on a migration strategy before any files are changed
Start direct execution on the first file, and let the pattern for the remaining 59 emerge as you go
Direct execution with a detailed upfront prompt specifying every file to change and in what order
Skip planning and directly write a single large commit converting all 60 files at once
questions.explanation: Multiple call-site patterns, an open strategic question (shim vs. big-bang), and 60 affected files are exactly the markers for plan mode: real architectural ambiguity that's cheaper to resolve before implementation than to discover mid-migration. Starting direct execution (B, C, D) assumes the approach is already settled when it explicitly isn't.

questions.question: While investigating how a legacy authentication module handles session expiry, you need to read a dozen files, trace several call paths, and grep for every caller of a deprecated function — all before you can even start deciding what to change. Why use the Explore subagent for this phase instead of doing it directly in the main conversation?
options: It isolates the verbose intermediate discovery output (file reads, grep hits) into a sub-agent, returning a summary and preserving main-conversation context for the actual decision-making and implementation
It is the only way to read more than 10 files in a single Claude Code session
It automatically writes unit tests for whatever code it discovers
It replaces the need for plan mode entirely once exploration is complete
questions.explanation: The Explore subagent's value is context preservation — verbose discovery work generates output that would otherwise crowd out the main conversation's budget. There's no hard 10-file limit it works around, it doesn't generate tests, and it complements plan mode's design phase rather than replacing it.

questions.question: A prose instruction — "format dates consistently" — has produced inconsistent results across several generated files: some use `MM/DD/YYYY`, others ISO 8601, others relative phrases like "3 days ago". What is the most effective next step to get consistent output?
options: Provide 2–3 concrete input/output examples showing exactly which raw date values should map to which formatted strings
Rewrite the instruction with more adjectives emphasizing that consistency really matters this time
Switch to a larger, more capable model so it infers the intended format on its own
Add a linter step after generation that rejects any non-ISO-8601 date and asks Claude to fix it via trial and error
questions.explanation: When prose is interpreted inconsistently, concrete input/output examples resolve the ambiguity directly — they show the exact transformation rather than describing it in words that can be read multiple ways. Emphatic rewording (B) doesn't add information. A bigger model (C) doesn't resolve an underspecified requirement. A post-hoc linter (D) is a much slower trial-and-error loop than just specifying the format upfront.

questions.question: Before implementing a rate-limiting feature for an internal API, in a domain you have limited experience with, what's the most effective way to surface open design questions (burst allowance, per-user vs. per-IP limits, behavior on limit breach) before code gets written?
options: Use the interview pattern — have Claude ask clarifying questions about the unresolved considerations before implementation begins
Implement the most common industry-standard rate-limiting approach immediately and adjust later if it's wrong
Write the code first, then write tests afterward that describe whatever behavior the code happens to have
Skip requirements gathering and let code review catch any missing considerations after the PR is opened
questions.explanation: The interview pattern — asking questions before implementing — is designed exactly for unfamiliar-domain situations where design considerations (burst handling, scope of limiting, breach behavior) aren't yet known. Implementing immediately (B) risks building the wrong thing. Writing tests to match whatever the code does (C) inverts test-driven iteration's purpose. Deferring to code review (D) is a much more expensive place to catch a fundamentally wrong design than before implementation starts.

questions.question: A migration script fails two independent-seeming checks: (1) it doesn't handle `NULL` values in the `plan` column, and (2) a log message has a typo. Separately, it has two interacting issues: (3) retry logic double-counts failed rows on timeout, and (4) the row counter isn't safe under concurrent retries. How should this feedback be delivered?
options: Report issues 3 and 4 together in one detailed message since they interact, and issues 1 and 2 can be handled sequentially since they're independent
Report all four issues strictly one at a time, regardless of whether they interact, to keep each round simple
Report all four issues in one message regardless of interaction, since more context is always better
Only report the highest-severity issue (3) and withhold the others until it's fixed
questions.explanation: Interacting issues (3 and 4) should be given together because fixing one changes the correct fix for the other — sequential fixing risks a first fix that has to be partially undone once the second issue's context lands. Independent issues (1 and 2) don't carry that risk, so sequential handling is fine and keeps each round simpler. Treating everything identically (B, C) ignores this distinction, and withholding known issues (D) just adds unnecessary iteration rounds.

questions.question: A CI pipeline step runs `claude "Summarize the risk of this database migration"` and the job never completes — it just sits until the runner times out. What is the correct fix?
options: Add the `-p` (`--print`) flag so Claude Code runs non-interactively and exits after producing output
Increase the CI runner's timeout so Claude Code has more time to respond
Pipe `/dev/null` into the command's stdin to simulate a closed terminal
Split the prompt into smaller chunks sent as separate CI steps
questions.explanation: Without `-p`/`--print`, Claude Code runs in interactive mode and waits for input that a CI job will never provide — this is a mode problem, not a timing problem, so increasing the timeout (B) just delays the same hang. Piping `/dev/null` (C) is a workaround around the real fix. Splitting the prompt (D) doesn't address why the process is waiting for input in the first place.

questions.question: A team wants their CI-invoked review step to produce individual, actionable inline PR comments — one per issue found, each tagged with file, line, and severity — rather than a single block of prose a human has to manually re-triage. Which combination of flags supports this?
options: `--output-format json` together with `--json-schema` pointing at a schema that defines fields like `file`, `line`, and `severity`
`--verbose` combined with `--pretty-print`
`-p` alone is sufficient; JSON structuring happens automatically once non-interactive mode is enabled
Piping the plain-text output through a separate regex-parsing script is the only supported approach
questions.explanation: `--output-format json` plus `--json-schema` is the documented mechanism for forcing structured, schema-conformant output your pipeline can parse programmatically into individual PR comments. `-p` alone only controls interactivity, not output structure. `--verbose`/`--pretty-print` aren't the relevant flags, and regex-parsing free text is a fragile fallback, not the supported approach.

questions.question: A Claude Code session generates a new payment-processing function, and the same session is then asked to review its own diff before opening the PR. The review comes back clean, but a human reviewer later finds a real edge-case bug. What does this scenario illustrate, and what's the better setup going forward?
options: The same session that generated the code is a structurally weaker reviewer of it — future reviews should run as an independent instance with no memory of the generation session, given only the diff
The review flag was probably misconfigured; re-running `--output-format json` in the same session would have caught the bug
Self-review is equally reliable as independent review; this was simply an unlucky miss
The fix is to have the same session review the diff three times in a row and take a majority vote
questions.explanation: A session that just wrote the code carries the same assumptions into reviewing it, making it prone to confirming its own reasoning rather than challenging it — an independent review instance, seeded only with the diff, is structurally better positioned to catch what the generating session missed. Re-running with a different output flag (B) doesn't address the underlying self-review weakness, calling it equally reliable (C) contradicts the observed miss, and repeating the same biased reviewer three times (D) doesn't introduce genuine independence.

questions.question: A team wants to package a multi-step dependency-audit procedure that bundles a reference checklist file, a couple of helper scripts, and frontmatter controlling which tools it may use and whether it runs in an isolated context. A plain slash command template can't hold this — scripts, reference material, and per-invocation tool restrictions. What should this be built as instead?
options: A skill (`.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`) — skills bundle instructions, scripts, and reference material with richer frontmatter (`context`, `allowed-tools`), unlike a slash command's simple prompt template.
A longer slash command with the scripts pasted inline as markdown code blocks.
A project-level CLAUDE.md section describing the procedure in prose.
A `.claude/rules/` file with a broad glob pattern matching every file in the repo.
questions.explanation: Skills are exactly for packaged capabilities that need bundled scripts, reference material, and per-invocation frontmatter controls like context isolation and tool restriction — a slash command is a simple prompt template without that structure. A prose CLAUDE.md section (C) isn't invoked on demand the way a skill is, and a broad rules file (D) is for always-applicable conventions, not an on-demand bundled procedure.

questions.question: A project ships `.claude/skills/code-audit/SKILL.md` with a checklist the whole team uses. One developer wants to tweak the checklist for their own workflow without changing what teammates get when they invoke the shared skill. What should they do?
options: Create `~/.claude/skills/code-audit/SKILL.md` under the same name — it overrides the skill only in their own sessions and never touches the shared, committed project version.
Directly edit the project's `.claude/skills/code-audit/SKILL.md` and simply avoid committing the change.
Rename their personal copy to `code-audit-v2` so it's a completely separate skill from the team's.
Ask every teammate to individually opt out of the shared skill in their own settings.
questions.explanation: A user-level skill under the same name overrides the project skill only in that developer's own sessions, leaving the shared, committed version untouched for everyone else. Editing the project file directly (B) risks an accidental commit or a working tree that silently diverges from what's checked in; renaming it (C) works but abandons the familiar `/code-audit` invocation for no benefit; asking teammates to opt out (D) solves the wrong problem entirely.

questions.question: A CI pipeline runs `claude -p` to generate new unit tests for a module that already has substantial test coverage. Generated tests keep duplicating scenarios that existing test files already cover. What is the most effective fix?
options: Provide the existing test files as context in the prompt/session, so Claude extends coverage instead of regenerating scenarios that already exist.
Lower the test-generation prompt's temperature so results are more consistent between runs.
Run the test-generation step twice and keep only tests that appear in both runs.
Switch to a larger model with a bigger context window so it can silently avoid duplicates on its own.
questions.explanation: Giving Claude the existing test files as context is what lets it extend coverage rather than regenerate scenarios it can't otherwise know already exist. Lower temperature (B) doesn't supply the missing information about what's already covered; running it twice (C) doesn't address the root cause either; and a bigger context window alone (D) doesn't guarantee the existing tests are actually included as context.

questions.question: An engineer is migrating a deprecated auth library across 40 files, several of which turn out to need different replacement logic depending on how they use the old library. After exploring in plan mode and settling on an approach, what should happen next?
options: Drop to direct execution to implement the now-settled plan — plan mode isn't an all-or-nothing mode for the whole session, it's for the investigation phase where design risk exists.
Stay in plan mode for the entire remaining session, including the file-by-file implementation, since the task started out complex.
Restart in a completely new session for the implementation phase, discarding the plan mode findings.
Ask the user to manually implement the plan themselves, since plan mode's job ends once a plan exists.
questions.explanation: A common, effective pattern is using plan mode for the investigation phase, then dropping to direct execution once the plan is settled and approved — plan mode is a phase for the parts of a task carrying real design risk, not a mode for the whole session regardless of what's left to do. Staying in plan mode throughout (B) adds unnecessary friction to implementation, and discarding the findings (C) or handing the plan to the user (D) both waste the investigation that was just done.

questions.question: A skill for filing a bug report needs the user to supply a ticket ID as an argument, and the team wants that expected argument to be shown rather than guessed at before the skill runs. Which frontmatter field addresses this?
options: `argument-hint`, which documents the expected argument (e.g., `[ticket-id]`) so it's shown rather than guessed at.
`context: fork`, since forking isolates the skill's execution context.
`allowed-tools`, since it restricts what actions the skill may take.
`description`, since a sufficiently detailed description implies what arguments are needed.
questions.explanation: `argument-hint` exists specifically to document the expected argument shape for a skill or command. `context: fork` (B) controls context isolation, not argument documentation; `allowed-tools` (C) restricts tool access, not arguments; and relying on `description` (D) to imply argument structure is indirect compared to a field built for exactly this purpose.
